elements.sent-2411-001: Benchmarking anomaly detection approaches for multivariate time series is challenging due to the lack of high-quality datasets.
elements.sent-2411-002: Current publicly available datasets are too small, not diverse and feature trivial anomalies, which hinders measurable progress in this research area.
elements.sent-2411-003: We propose a solution: a diverse, extensive, and non-trivial dataset generated via state-of-the-art simulation tools that reflects realistic behaviour of an automotive powertrain, including its multivariate, dynamic and variable-state properties.
elements.sent-2411-004: To cater for both unsupervised and semi-supervised anomaly detection settings, as well as time series generation and forecasting, we make different versions of the dataset available, where training and test subsets are offered in contaminated and clean versions, depending on the task.
elements.sent-2411-005: We also provide baseline results from a small selection of approaches based on deterministic and variational autoencoders, as well as a non-parametric approach.
elements.sent-2411-006: As expected, the baseline experimentation shows that the approaches trained on the semi-supervised version of the dataset outperform their unsupervised counterparts, highlighting a need for approaches more robust to contaminated training data.
elements.sent-2411-007: Copyright may be transferred without notice, after which this version may no longer be accessible.
elements.sent-2411-008: As the digitisation of industrial processes progresses, more and more data is recorded.
elements.sent-2411-009: Ensuring this data is representative of the process is important, as downstream tasks like modelling or optimisation can be negatively impacted by incomplete or contaminated data.
elements.sent-2411-010: For tasks that require system behaviour modelling, data deviating from the norm is hence undesired, and we speak of anomalous behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-011: Recorded data manifests itself in many forms depending on the application and domain, one form being time series.
elements.sent-2411-012: Examples of real-world time series applications are diverse, ranging from cardiology~\cite{moody_impact_2001 and server metrics monitoring~\cite{su_robust_2019 to water systems~\cite{mathur_swat_2016, ahmed_wadi_2017, zhang_anomaly_2023 and unmanned aerial vehicles~\cite{zhong_unmanned_2022 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Note that, we use time series and sequences synonymously throughout this paper.
elements.sent-2411-014: Time series are signals that represent a property or feature of a dynamic system as a function of time, usually sampled at a fixed rate.
elements.sent-2411-015: An arbitrary time series $\mathcal{S}$ can be univariate, i.e.\ $\mathcal{S \in \mathbb{R ^{T}$, or multivariate, i.e.\ $\mathcal{S \in \mathbb{R ^{T \times d}$, where $T$ refers to the number of discrete time steps and $d$ to the number of features in the time series.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: More specifically, univariate time series solely possess a temporal correlation, i.e.\ along the time axis, whereas multivariate time series can also contain correlation along the feature axis.
elements.sent-2411-017: Detecting anomalous behaviour in time series is referred to time series anomaly detection, which can be split into two main areas: continuous- and discrete-sequence~\cite{correia_online_2024 , where the former is the most common type present in public datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-018: It is defined as detecting anomalies in a process that runs for a continuous time period without breaks.
elements.sent-2411-019: Typically, the test subset $\mathcal{D ^\text{test}$ in the dataset $\mathcal{D}$ in a continuous-sequence problem consists of a singular multivariate time series composed of multiple nominal and anomalous sub-sequences, i.e.\ $\mathcal{D ^\text{test = \{\mathcal{S _1\}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: In this work, we use nominal as a synonym for normal or anomaly-free to avoid confusion with Gaussian distributions.
elements.sent-2411-021: Discrete-sequence anomaly detection, in contrast, is defined as detecting anomalies in $N$ chunks of processes that happen independently of each other, such as automotive test benches, where several tests may occur sequentially but are not temporally contiguous and hence provide a time series for each test, i.e.\ $\mathcal{D ^\text{test = \{\mathcal{S _1, ..., \mathcal{S _n, ..., \mathcal{S _{N}\}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Here, the testees, i.e.\ the test subjects, are not monitored over a continuous period of time, but are instead monitored solely during each process chunk.
elements.sent-2411-023: Automotive testing is not the only use for discrete-sequence anomaly detection, however.
elements.sent-2411-024: Another discrete-sequence problem could also include the analysis of the flight behaviour of an aeroplane, where the time while it is docked is irrelevant and may not be recorded.
elements.sent-2411-025: Therefore, datasets for discrete-sequence anomaly detection consist of several nominal and anomalous time series, where a given anomalous time series may be entirely anomalous or only partly.
elements.sent-2411-026: Depending on the system, the time series data may also feature variable states, meaning the recorded signals appear slightly different if certain external conditions change but are still considered nominal.
elements.sent-2411-027: One example of a variable-state system is a battery, where the voltage response to current changes depending on states like the battery temperature and the battery state of charge (SoC).
elements.sent-2411-028: A problem involving such a system requires the distinction between behaviour changes due to different states and behaviour changes due to an anomaly, further complicating detection.
elements.sent-2411-029: In addition to that, detecting anomalous behaviour in a timely manner is also advantageous because the source of anomalous behaviour may bring about damage to said system.
elements.sent-2411-030: Such problems where the detection delay plays a role are referred to as online time series anomaly detection.
elements.sent-2411-031: Analogous to types of learning, there is supervised, semi-supervised, and unsupervised anomaly detection.
elements.sent-2411-032: Supervised anomaly detection is essentially imbalanced binary time series classification and is only rarely found in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-033: This is most likely because, in real-world use cases, possible anomaly types are rarely known a priori.
elements.sent-2411-034: In addition to that, labelling data is expensive, which is why unsupervised and semi-supervised anomaly detection are more relevant in both literature and the real world.
elements.sent-2411-035: Unsupervised anomaly detection is independent of any labels, i.e.\ any available data for model training contains both anomalous and nominal time series, and it is not known which is which~\cite{chandola_anomaly_2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-036: In contrast, semi-supervised anomaly detection can be considered a more relaxed setting, where anomalous time series are absent from the training subset~\cite{chandola_anomaly_2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-037: In the real world, semi-supervised problems still require some labelling to ensure an entirely nominal training subset, which is not always given.
elements.sent-2411-038: Recently, time series anomaly detection has mostly been attempted using deep learning, with simpler statistical methods remaining largely unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-039: Several researchers~\cite{wu_current_2021, audibert_deep_2022, rewicki_is_2023 have hence raised concerns on whether deep learning-based methods are warranted for the complexity of publicly available datasets and even challenge any anomaly detection performance claims made because such datasets are deemed as flawed~\cite{wu_current_2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: The lack of a standardised procedure to benchmark approaches on a high-quality dataset is considered a fundamental issue, which prevents any tangible and objective advances in the field.
elements.sent-2411-041: Our contribution is a novel, non-trivial, and high-quality dataset consisting of multivariate time series for online anomaly detection, named the Powertrain Anomaly Time series bencHmark (PATH) dataset.
elements.sent-2411-042: While primarily aimed at unsupervised anomaly detection, we provide versions for semi-supervised anomaly detection and time series generation and forecasting as well.
elements.sent-2411-043: Despite the data being generated using simulation, the electric vehicle simulation model is strongly motivated by the real world and is therefore complex and variable-state.
elements.sent-2411-044: This paper is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-045: First, we introduce the related work in the area of benchmarking time series anomaly detection approaches.
elements.sent-2411-046: It includes discussion on the datasets used to evaluate time series anomaly detection approaches in the past, why they are no longer suitable, and a summary of the work dedicated to outlining the status quo in benchmarking time series anomaly detection approaches.
elements.sent-2411-047: Then, we introduce the PATH dataset in detail, outlining the generation process and a few benchmarking considerations.
elements.sent-2411-048: Following that, we provide some baseline results for a selection of deep learning-based approaches, as well as a non-parametric method.
elements.sent-2411-049: Finally, we conclude our work and outline an outlook on future work.
elements.sent-2411-050: The source code corresponding to this paper and the simulation model can be found under \url{https://github.com/lcs-crr/PATH , and the dataset can be downloaded from \url{https://zenodo.org/records/13255121 \cite{data_set .}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Over the last few years, five benchmark datasets have emerged as by far the most popular, with at least one of them being cited in the vast majority of publications on multivariate time series anomaly detection.
elements.sent-2411-052: A summary of the properties of these datasets is shown in Table~\ref{tab:public_data_set_properties .}

elements.sent-2411-053: The MSL~\cite{hundman_detecting_2018 , SMAP~\cite{hundman_detecting_2018 , and SMD~\cite{su_robust_2019 have already been thoroughly analysed by Wu and Keogh~\cite{wu_current_2021 , who point out several issues with the datasets.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: The first issue observed in the datasets is triviality, defined by being solvable using so-called one-line code, such as the moving standard deviation over a subset of the dataset features.
elements.sent-2411-055: Moreover, all of them suffer from what Wu and Keogh~\cite{wu_current_2021 have called unrealistic anomaly density, meaning that they have sub-sequences with a very high anomaly share and hence do not match the assumption that anomalies are rare events.}
elements.sent-2411-056: In addition to that, Wu and Keogh~\cite{wu_current_2021 suspect possible mislabelling present in the MSL dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-057: They base their suspicion on the fact that the dataset contains sub-sequences with static behaviour in an evidently dynamic channel, which is labelled as nominal.
elements.sent-2411-058: Many of the issues pointed out by Wu and Keogh~\cite{wu_current_2021 can also be extended to the SWaT and WADI datasets, as discussed by Correia et al.~\cite{correia_online_2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Certain real-world applications like automotive testing present complexities previously unseen in public datasets.
elements.sent-2411-060: As outlined by Correia et al.~\cite{correia_tevae_2024 , such applications comprise much more diverse discrete-sequence datasets, owed to the presence of both highly dynamic and mostly static features, as well as variable states.}
elements.sent-2411-061: The presence of variable states leads to features exhibiting a different pattern depending on the time it is observed.
elements.sent-2411-062: In the context of automotive testing, an example of such a feature would be the state of charge of a battery, which discharges with time and hence shows different behaviour for the same test done twice in a row.
elements.sent-2411-063: Hence, we construct a new high-quality dataset that is non-trivial and reflects real-world complexity.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Doubts Regarding Applicability of Deep Learning
elements.sent-2411-064: Recently, there has been growing doubt on whether deep learning (DL) algorithms are definitively the better choice for time series anomaly detection.
elements.sent-2411-065: For the purpose of this publication, classical methods refer to all approaches not based on DL, including non-parametric and statistical approaches, as well as simpler machine learning methods like clustering.
elements.sent-2411-066: Wu and Keogh~\cite{wu_current_2021 claim that the superiority of DL in anomaly detection is assumed to be a given, despite a lack of clear evidence for the need for DL.}
elements.sent-2411-067: They stress that existing classical methods should be considered, given their generally simpler and faster nature.
elements.sent-2411-068: To investigate the comparative performance of classical methods and DL-based methods, Audibert et al.~\cite{audibert_deep_2022 analyse a variety of different models on five of the most popular benchmark datasets, shown in Table~\ref{tab:public_data_set_properties .}}
elements.sent-2411-069: They conclude that, across the datasets considered, there is no algorithm that dominates all the other ones, arguing that there is no reason to omit classical methods from benchmarking.
elements.sent-2411-070: Rewicki et al.~\cite{rewicki_is_2023 also conduct a comparative study of classical and DL-based methods, though on the UCR Anomaly Archive benchmark proposed by Wu and Keogh~\cite{wu_current_2021 , which exclusively contains univariate time series and therefore lacks correlations between channels present in multivariate time series.}}
elements.sent-2411-071: They conclude that classical methods perform better than their DL counterparts, although this is to be expected given the simpler, univariate nature of the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-072: While the findings and doubts of the above-mentioned are valid, they are limited by the lack of large, high-quality multivariate datasets.
elements.sent-2411-073: In this paper, we purposefully include results from a state-of-the-art classical method to find out whether doubts on DL are still justified for extensive and complex real-world datasets.
elements.sent-2411-074: See Section~\ref{sec:results for results and discussion.}
elements.sent-2411-075: To create an extensive and diverse dataset, we propose to use a physically-inspired model from which we can generate data using simulation.
elements.sent-2411-076: MathWorks offers reference models for a variety of dynamic systems, one of which is the full electric vehicle (FEV) model~\cite{mathworks_build_2024 from the powertrain block set in Simulink.}
elements.sent-2411-077: This choice is based on our familiarity with the domain, as generating data blindly without any background may lead to systematic errors.
elements.sent-2411-078: The FEV model offered by MathWorks consists of six main subsystems: the drive cycle block, the driver block, the environment block, the controllers block and the vehicle block.
elements.sent-2411-079: The topology of the FEV model is illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:sim_model .}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.70\textwidth]{SimModel.eps} \caption{Simplified schematic of the FEV model used for the generation of the PATH dataset. Numbers represent the indices of signal flow, reference is shown in Table~\ref{tab:signals}.} \label{fig:sim_model}
elements.sent-2411-080: To represent system behaviour, $d_\mathcal{D =16$ signals are chosen to be logged during simulation and are summarised in Table~\ref{tab:signals .}}
elements.sent-2411-081: We chose these signals based on domain knowledge, with the goal of picking the features that are most representative of powertrain behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-082: The drive cycle block of the FEV model defines the target vehicle speed and features a series of real-world drive cycles, i.e.\ profiles depicting the target vehicle speed as a function of time.
elements.sent-2411-083: From the list of speed profiles available in the original FEV model~\cite{mathworks_build_2024 , a subset of them is eliminated due to their unrealistic nature, e.g.\ high linearity or duplicity, as many cycles are present as sub-sequences in others.}
elements.sent-2411-084: Our analysis shows that, for example, the presence of the FTP72 drive cycle within FTP75 or the presence of the LA92Short drive cycle within LA92.
elements.sent-2411-085: In addition to that, drive cycles aimed at heavy vehicles, like trucks or buses, are also eliminated.
elements.sent-2411-086: The resulting subset of drive cycles chosen for simulation contains $33$ different speed profiles of varying length, shown in Table~\ref{tab:drive_cycles , along with their lengths in seconds.}
elements.sent-2411-087: Some drive cycles may be designed for specific types of powertrains such as diesel ones, but given that they only represent vehicle speed profiles, there is no reason why they cannot be driven by a vehicle with an electric powertrain, like the one modelled in this case.

elements.sent-2411-088: The driver block of the FEV model regulates the dynamic system to maintain the target speed.
elements.sent-2411-089: Its inputs are the target vehicle speed and the actual vehicle speed, and its outputs are the acceleration and deceleration control commands, index $12$ and $13$ in Table~\ref{tab:signals , respectively, which are fed into the controllers block of the FEV model.}
elements.sent-2411-090: This block takes said accelerator and brake pedal commands stemming as well as vehicle states like actual vehicle speed, electric motor speed and battery signals to calculate request signals for the powertrain, like the required electric motor torque and the brake signal, as well as battery management system signals like the battery SoC, index $5$ in Table~\ref{tab:signals .}
elements.sent-2411-091: Electric vehicles are capable of regenerative braking, meaning, the electric motor is used to decelerate the vehicle by acting as a generator, thereby charging the battery if it is not already fully charged.
elements.sent-2411-092: Following is the vehicle block of the FEV model, which outputs how the vehicle reacts to any inputs and contains the electric plant subsystem and the drivetrain subsystem.
elements.sent-2411-093: Both take inputs from the controllers block, including the battery SoC, and the environment block, as well as from each other, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:veh_model .}
elements.sent-2411-094: The electric plant subsystem outputs the electric motor torque, the battery current and power, the battery temperature and the cooling pump and refrigerator powers, which correspond to indices $2$, $6$, $7$, $14$, $15$ and $16$ in Table~\ref{tab:signals , respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-095: The electric motor torque is input into the drivetrain subsystem, which in turn outputs the electric motor speed, and front and rear axle torques, forces and speeds, corresponding to indices $1$, $3$, $4$, $8$, $9$, $10$, $11$ in Table~\ref{tab:signals , respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-096: The motor speed is also fed back into the electric plant model, completing the control loop.
elements.sent-2411-097: Both subsystems also contain further subsystems within them which uncover the causal relationships between their respective signals, and diving as deep as the lowest abstraction level of the model is outside the scope of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-098: Readers interested in more detail can refer to the Simulink model available in the provided repository.
elements.sent-2411-099: By default, the battery model features a static temperature model, however, to increase system complexity, a dynamic temperature model is added to the FEV model.
elements.sent-2411-100: The model used is the EV Battery Cooling System~\cite{mathworks_ev_2024 , also from MathWorks.}

Output signals of the vehicle model, which are not fed back into other subsystems, are not shown, for simplicity.} \label{fig:veh_model}
elements.sent-2411-101: The environment block of the FEV model dictates environmental conditions that affect the longitudinal dynamics of the FEV model.
elements.sent-2411-102: Parameters like atmospheric pressure, wind speed, road grade and coefficient of friction can be set within this subsystem.
elements.sent-2411-103: By default, the signals are logged at a sampling frequency of \qty{10 {\hertz and the solver used is the differential algebraic equations' solver for Simscape (daessc).}}
elements.sent-2411-104: Physical simulations may encounter numerical under- and overflow, which slow down simulations drastically.
elements.sent-2411-105: To avoid this, a timeout counter of one hour is set in place to skip the current simulation if triggered.
elements.sent-2411-106: Simulation time depends on the length of the drive cycle, however, for the computer hardware used simulations never take longer than 20 minutes, and hence one hour is considered sufficient for problem-free simulations.
elements.sent-2411-107: To generate a dataset that is not only extensive but also diverse, $100$ simulations have been undertaken for each of the $33$ drive cycles, each with random initial battery temperatures and battery SoCs.
elements.sent-2411-108: At this stage, all model properties have been left as default, and hence all simulation results have been considered nominal.
elements.sent-2411-109: For each simulation, the two states (battery temperatures and battery SoCs) have been sampled from uniform distributions $\mathcal{U (\qty{10 {\degreeCelsius , \qty{30 {\degreeCelsius )$ and $\mathcal{U (\qty{10 {\percent , \qty{100 {\percent )$, respectively, to ensure no bias is introduced.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: Sampling from uniform distributions also eliminates and reduces the effectiveness of simple threshold and control chart methods because the battery temperature and state of charge, but also, by extension, other channels, exhibit nominal but high deviation from the average behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-111: As a precautionary measure, drive cycles with a minimum SoC value lower than \qty{5 {\percent have been removed, as very low values have been observed to result in abnormal behaviour.}}
elements.sent-2411-112: After simulation, $N_\text{n =3273$ highly diverse and unique nominal time series have been collected.}

elements.sent-2411-114: For the generation of anomalous time series, six types of anomalies have been considered.
elements.sent-2411-115: Some types can occur as both sub-sequence anomalies and sequence anomalies~\cite{correia_online_2024 , while others only in sequence anomaly form, due to simulation model limitations.}
elements.sent-2411-116: To better distinguish the two anomaly forms, we refer to sequence anomalies as whole-sequence anomalies henceforth.
elements.sent-2411-117: The distribution of sub-sequence and whole-sequence anomalies across the different anomaly types is shown in Table~\ref{tab:anomaly_types .}
elements.sent-2411-118: Anomalies are caused by changing certain model properties prior to simulation, ensuring that any observed anomalous behaviour results from simulation rather than manual tampering of the data, like in the UCR dataset~\cite{dau_ucr_2019 , which eliminates any bias.}
elements.sent-2411-119: We ran all simulations with a fixed seed of $1$.

elements.sent-2411-120: For the first kind of anomaly, we turn the regenerative braking off, which leads to visibly different motor and axle torques, as well as battery current and power, as these can no longer assume negative values.
elements.sent-2411-121: When regenerative braking is off, the battery SoC now has an exclusively negative gradient as it is no longer recharged via regenerative braking, and hence it decreases at a faster rate.
elements.sent-2411-122: The brake pedal is also used more to compensate for the missing braking motor torque.
elements.sent-2411-123: For each of the cycles, this anomaly type is simulated in two different ways: without regenerative braking from the beginning and from a random point in time within the drive cycle.
elements.sent-2411-124: This random point in time is sampled from a uniform distribution $\mathcal{U (0.2T, 0.8T)$, where $T$ denotes the temporal length of the drive cycle in question, see Table~\ref{tab:drive_cycles .}}
elements.sent-2411-125: This statistical distribution is used for all sub-sequence anomaly types.

elements.sent-2411-233: We propose a novel multivariate time series dataset for online anomaly detection, called the Powertrain Anomaly Time series bencHmark (PATH) dataset.
elements.sent-2411-234: The PATH dataset is generated using simulation, where the model it is based on resembles a real-world dynamic system.
elements.sent-2411-235: In addition to that, simulation is done in a variety of different initial states to further add to the diversity of the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-236: To increase the complexity of the dataset, noise is applied and the beginning of time series are randomly trimmed.
elements.sent-2411-237: The anomalies in the PATH dataset arise from changing parameters prior to simulation, as opposed to manual data manipulation, resulting in anomalies that are non-trivial and realistic.
elements.sent-2411-238: We offer the dataset in three different versions: one for unsupervised anomaly detection, where the training subset consists of both anomalous and nominal sequences, another for semi-supervised anomaly detection, where the training subset consists of nominal sequences only, and one for time series generation or forecasting, where both the training and test subsets are nominal.
elements.sent-2411-239: Lastly, for each of the versions, we offer three different folds with a pre-defined train and test split to ensure generalised and comparable results.
elements.sent-2411-240: The experiments conducted in this work further support the claim of non-triviality because, even when the threshold choice is removed as a factor, the best approach in an unsupervised setting only manages to achieve an $F_1$ score of $0.56$ and an average detection delay of \qty{651.0 {\second .}}
elements.sent-2411-241: In contrast, however, the results significantly improve when the clean version of the test subset is used.
elements.sent-2411-242: Here, the average theoretical best $F_1$ score reaches $0.83$ and an average detection delay of \qty{383.9 {\second , highlighting the need for methods more robust to anomalous data in the unlabelled and contaminated training subset.}}

elements.sent-2411-245: Battery ageing can be characterised by the charge capacity, which, fixed in this dataset, can be changed dynamically to simulate battery ageing, which will have an impact on the entire system.
elements.sent-2411-246: This property can be especially useful for research in the area of unsupervised predictive maintenance, where an explicit health signal is not present.

elements.sent-2411-250: We also provide baseline results from a selection of approaches based on deterministic and variational autoencoders, as well as a non-parametric approach.
elements.sent-2411-251: Detecting anomalous behaviour in time series is referred to time series anomaly detection, which can be split into two main areas: continuous- and discrete-sequence~\cite{correia_online_2024 , where the former is \lc{the only type addressed in public datasets.}}
elements.sent-2411-252: Continuous-sequence problems are defined as detecting anomalies in a process that runs for a continuous time period without breaks.
elements.sent-2411-253: Discrete-sequence anomaly detection, in contrast, is defined as detecting anomalies in $N$ chunks of processes that happen independently of each other.
elements.sent-2411-254: Problems where the detection delay plays a role \lc{require evaluation of the time series before it ends and are referred to as online time series anomaly detection.}
elements.sent-2411-255: This is most likely because, in real-world problems, possible anomaly types \lc{and how they manifest themselves in the data are rarely known a priori.}
elements.sent-2411-256: Moreover, labelling data is expensive, which is why unsupervised and semi-supervised anomaly detection are more prevalent in literature and relevant to the real world.
elements.sent-2411-257: According to Schmidl et al.~\cite{schmidlAnomalyDetectionTime2022 , unsupervised anomaly detection involves detecting anomalous behaviour without a training procedure.}
elements.sent-2411-258: This definition implies that learning nominal behaviour from mostly-nominal data is not possible, which we \lc{challenge in this paper.}
elements.sent-2411-259: Our contribution is a non-trivial, and high-quality \lc{discrete-sequence dataset consisting of multivariate time series for online anomaly detection, named the Powertrain Anomaly Time series bencHmark (PATH) dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-260: It includes discussion on the datasets used to evaluate time series anomaly detection approaches in the past, and a summary of the work dedicated to outlining the status quo in benchmarking time series anomaly detection approaches.
elements.sent-2411-261: Following that, we provide some baseline results for a selection of deep learning-based models, as well as a non-parametric approach.
